# Welcome {.unnumbered}

Welcome to SKL402 — Introduction to R. This is a digital seminar designed as a fundamental skills module for the *Master in Economics and Business Administration* (MØA) at the Norwegian School of Economics. The course will take you from zero to a working knowledge of R programming, covering everything from installation and basic programming to data wrangling, visualisation, and reporting. The content of the course has been selected to serve as a prerequisite for other courses at NHH that use R as their programming language. The aim is not to make you a master of R, but to bring you to a working level — operational enough to quickly find your footing and take on substantial analytical tasks in other courses across the programme.

R is a free, open-source programming language built specifically for statistical computing and data analysis. First released in 1993 and maintained by a global community, it has become one of the most widely used tools in economics, finance, and the social sciences. Its strength lies in a rich ecosystem of up-to-date packages that can be easily installed on top of the language itself and that extend it to almost any analytical task. Throughout this course we will explore some of the most central and user-friendly third-party packages for working with, analysing, and visualising data. Because R is both powerful and freely available, it is widely used in science, business, the public sector, and beyond. Think of R as the Swiss Army knife of data analysis that can get you out of just about any tight spot, as long as you know how to use it.

<!-- Video 1.1: Welcome -->
*Video will be embedded here.*

## Course overview

The course is organised into five topics:

1. **Getting Started and Working Effectively** — Installing R, navigating files, writing scripts, and understanding basic data structures.
2. **Programming Fundamentals** — Logical operators, control flow, loops, and writing your own functions.
3. **Data Wrangling** — Importing, cleaning, and transforming data with dplyr.
4. **Graphics** — Building publication-ready visualisations with ggplot2.
5. **Analysis, Reporting, and Wrap-up** — Linear regression, exporting results, Quarto reports, debugging, and using AI responsibly.

## How the course works

The material is presented as short video lessons, each covering a single topic. The videos are followed by a series of programming problems of varying difficulty, and it is *crucial* that you spend time on these problems in order to develop your own muscle memory and to get a sense of where you are in your own learning process. **The course material is self-contained, which means that you can start learning whenever and wherever you want.**

## Using Artificial Intelligence while learning

There is an important difference between *learning* to program and programming efficiently as a professional. Once you know a language well, AI tools can be a powerful way to write code faster and handle routine work. But that is not what this course is about. Here, the goal is for *you* to build real understaning and skills, and that only comes from working through problems yourself, making mistakes, and using your own brain to figure out why something does or does not work.

For that reason, you should not lean on AI to write your code for you while you are learning. Used carelessly, it deprives you of the incredibly valuable, but sometimes uncomfortable struggle that builds lasting skill. Used carefully, however, AI can be a valuable learning companion: a patient tutor to help you make sense of a confusing error message, talk through a difficult problem, or explain an unfamiliar concept in a different way. The distinction is whether the AI is doing the thinking for you, or helping you do your own thinking in a better way.

All students at NHH have access to a privacy-friendly AI assistant through [Sikt KI](https://ki.sikt.no/en), which you are welcome to use as a learning companion in this spirit.

## Where to get up-to-date information

This web-page only contains the contents of the course, in the form of video lessons and accompanying problem sets. For semester-specific information such as announcements, dates and locations for physical learning sessions, you should consult the course's Canvas page, to which you will gain access once you have completed your semester registration.

For exam dates, see the [official schedule information from NHH](https://www.nhh.no/en/for-students/schedule/).
